category: AML - shell company layering and beneficial-ownership obfuscation
risk_signals: Company with no genuine operations, employees, or physical presence moving significant funds
Complex, multi-layered ownership that obscures the ultimate beneficial owner
Round-sum transfers and turnover inconsistent with any real business activity
analyst_reasoning: Shell companies exist to move and layer value while hiding who ultimately controls it. Because the entity is legally real but economically empty, the red flags are the absence of substance (no staff, no premises, no trade rationale) combined with ownership layering designed to defeat beneficial-ownership checks.
investigation_steps: Establish whether the entity has real operations: premises, employees, tax and trade footprint
Unwind the ownership chain to identify the ultimate beneficial owner
Test transaction rationale - do flows match any plausible business purpose
decision: Escalate for enhanced due diligence, demand beneficial-ownership evidence, and file an STR if substance cannot be established.
outcome: Shell layers make funds hard to attribute; piercing beneficial ownership is the key to unwinding them.
lessons_learned: Economic emptiness (no staff, premises, or trade) is the defining shell-company red flag
Ownership layering is designed to defeat beneficial-ownership checks and must be unwound
Transaction purpose that matches no real business is decisive
